behaviors.description: The traveling-salesman heuristic produces a route that is no more than 1.5x the length of the optimal route for inputs up to 20 stops.
behaviors.justification: This is primarily algorithm-quality validation at a single solver boundary with no required I/O. Real failures here are heuristic logic regressions (ordering, cost accumulation, pruning), which are catchable by direct function tests against known optimal benchmarks.
behaviors.test_description: Call the heuristic directly with deterministic distance matrices/coordinate sets and compare route length to known optimum datasets (including cases up to 20 stops). Assert heuristic_length <= 1.5 * optimal_length for each fixture.

behaviors.description: The delivery time window estimates are computed correctly from the distance between consecutive stops and the configured average speed for the road type.
behaviors.justification: The core failure boundary is arithmetic/rules logic (time = distance/speed plus window accumulation). No real OS/network/subprocess dependency is required if distances and road-type speeds are provided as inputs.
behaviors.test_description: Invoke the estimator with explicit segment distances and road-type speed config, then assert per-stop ETA/time-window outputs against hand-calculated expectations, including mixed road types and rounding/precision edges.

behaviors.description: The shift-hours validator rejects a route whose total estimated time exceeds the driver's remaining shift hours, returning the excess amount.
behaviors.justification: This is a local validation rule (comparison and delta calculation). The real failure is logic-level (threshold handling, off-by-one, unit conversion), not infrastructure-level.
behaviors.test_description: Call the validator with total_estimated_time and remaining_shift_hours inputs around boundary values (equal, slightly under, slightly over). Assert rejection occurs only when exceeded and excess is exact.

behaviors.description: When one address in the list fails geocoding (API returns no results), the service skips that stop and optimizes the remaining addresses, reporting which addresses were skipped.
behaviors.justification: The failure appears at module handoff boundaries: geocoding result handling, filtering invalid stops, rerunning optimization, and reporting skipped addresses. A pure unit test of one function can miss cross-module state/contract issues.
behaviors.test_description: Wire service modules together with a controllable geocoder stub that returns no result for one address and valid coordinates for others. Execute the full service call and assert: skipped list contains the failed address, optimizer input excludes it, and returned route covers only valid stops.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses in the input list are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice.
behaviors.justification: This behavior spans deduplication + item aggregation + routing consumption. The real bug risk is in data handoff (e.g., dedup works but optimizer still receives duplicates, or items are dropped when merged).
behaviors.test_description: Run the service through input normalization into route generation with duplicated addresses carrying distinct items. Assert output has one stop for that address, merged item payload is complete, and route visits that coordinate once.
